headlines.title: A Rational View
headlines.fullText:
One hundred and thirty-seven years having elapsed since the adoption of the Declaration of Independence, the New York Sun drifts into reflective mood and turns out this national bit of optimism:
"Approximately 34,000 men have been members of the house of representatives. Among them have been some knaves and some fools, but never has the house as a body been more knavish or more foolish than the nation whose purse strings it holds. The senate has not escaped human weakness and infirmities, yet it has been the home of great and good men, serving with honor the best interests of their country, according to the light they had. The governors and the members of the legislatures taken generally, in their motives and acts, disclose no disquieting message to the intelligent. Bad men have reached high office; base conduct has not been unknown, but wholesome ambitions sustained by honorable works have been the general rule, and pettiness, corruption and wrongdoing have not been characteristic of the administration of the states."
In conclusion the Sun sends forth this ray of cheerfulness and hope:
"There is no reason why an American should hang his head today. His country, after an infancy menaced by many dangers, has achieved a youthful maturity of power and enlightenment. It holds the promise of a future of glory and humane accomplishment, and its natural resources and its noble history are entrusted to citizens not deficient in the qualities necessary for the continuation in dignity of its remarkable progress."
Certainly the country isn't going to wreck and ruin as fast as demagogues and muckrakers are pleased to forecast with yowl and screech. A little old-fashioned common sense, and steady application to the daily task, will keep the governor belt from slipping for quite a spell. Meantime, let Texas grow.

headlines.title: The Philippines
headlines.subtitle: XXX .- AGUINALDO'S INSURRECTION.
headlines.fullText:
BY Frederie J. Haskin.
General Merritt went so far as to appoint a commission to meet a similar body appointed by Aguinaldo to "confer with regard to the situation of affairs and to arrive at a mutual understanding of the intent, purposes, aims and desires of the Filipino people and of the people of the United States," Six sessions were held, the last occurring on Jan. 29, 1899, six days before the outbreak of open hostilities. Members of the conference went by Aguinaldo were either unable or unwilling to do anything definite. At the close of the last session they were again assured by the Americans that no form of hostilities would be begun by them.
By this time the insurgents had 10,000 militia in the very heart of the capital city of Manila itself. Pilar was in command. He schemed with rebel leaders without the city by day and plotted with his own lieutenants within the city by night. It had long been agreed that the rebels should rise the instant that rockets were sent up. This was expected at any moment in view of their studied policy of personal abuse heaped upon any and all American soldiers and officers.
The Americans, obeying strict orders, did not return physical retaliation for the rude jostling and vile words that harassed them day after day, both inside and outside the city. The Filipinos then concluded that the Americans were cowards and could be easily defeated. Open assaults on members of the Nebraska volunteer regiment, which was then in camp on the east line of Santa Mesa on the outskirts of Manila, and attempts to push back our outposts at the San Juan bridge, which divides the city of Manila from the province of Rizal, were common occurrences right up to the opening battle on the night of Feb. 4. A Filipino lieutenant had been in the habit of threatening the American sentry at this post, which was No. 2. On this particular night he approached in the darkness with a detail of six men. Private Grayson, Company D, First Nebraska volunteers, was on duty there. The native party continued to advance despite repeated challenges. Grayson then fired, killing the lieutenant. That shot started the insular war.
The Filipinos sent rockets aloft at once, the other pickets of the American outpost No. 2 fired a volley, which was returned by the natives on the other side of the San Juan river. Firing then became general, all along the line. This fusilade continued until midnight, and there were a number of skirmishes.
There was a lull until 4 a. m., when the Filipinos again began firing, extending their line of fire until it encircled Manila. This fire was returned, supplemented at last by a concerted advance movement which drove the rebels back several miles to Caloocan and other points. The American loss was not heavy, but the Filipinos suffered casualties of several hundred in dead and wounded. Two days later word was received that the United States senate had finally ratified the Treaty of Paris, which formally turned the islands over to the United States for the sum of $20,000,000 United States currency.
The next movement came on the night of Feb 22, when 500 insurgents invaded Tondo, a densely populated district of Manila. They set fire to the houses of their own people and fired on the American sentries. They expected the local militia to assist them, and the plan was to massacre all the whites, and some of the most fanatic wanted to treat the thousands of mestizos (half caste Filipino-Spaniards) in the same way. An exceedingly vigorous counter attack directed by Provost Marshal General Hughes resulted in the speedy retreat of the force, with whom the militia in the city failed to co-operate at that time.
In the meantime President McKinley had sent out the Schurmann commission in an attempt to find out just what the situation was. They reached Manila on March 4 and found a very bad state of affairs. They themselves were guarded constantly. Incendiary fires occurred daily. The streets were almost deserted, and filth and disease reigned more absolute than ever before. Half of the native population had taken to the woods long ago, and those who remained behind did so behind closed windows and barred doors. Nobody knew who was who. The few Filipinos who dared espouse the American cause were in hourly fear of assassination. With their assistance, the commission drew up and issued the proclamation of explanation and amity of April 4, which is supposed to have had considerable to do with the ultimate establishment of peace. This point has always been disputed, however. Most military men who were in the islands at that time contend that the early activities of both the Schurmann and Taft commissions, instead of doing great good, operated to prevent the army from speedily terminating the insurrection.
It has always been maintained by the Schurmann commission and its many followers that their proclamations and subsequent trips to different parts of the islands prevented the revolution from becoming a national one. Certainty it is true that organized revolution made headway only on the island of Luzon. Here it appears to have been confined to the Tagalog provinces of Manila, Cavite, Batangas, Laguna, Nueva Ecija, Zambales and the districts of Morfong, Principe and Infanta. In the other provinces the rebels were hated and feared, for the people there suffered greater taxation and more personal abuse from the Aguinaldo "government" than they ever had from the Spaniards. Some went so far as to appeal to the Americans for help. The disturbances among the Visayans and the Moros of Mindanao were local affairs, due to brigandage, and were in no way connected with the Tagalog revolt.
Matters drifted along month after month. Both armies operated against each other constantly, but mostly in scattered engagements. The Filipinos seldom won, though they proved brave and resourceful foes. The delays and interruptions were to the advantage of the Americans, as troops were being landed in shiploads nearly every week or so. A decisive victory was scored in December by Gen. MacArthur in the capture of Malolos, the headquarters of Aguinaldo. A stiff resistance was offered by General Luna, one of his tactics being the burning of entire villages of his own people. The object of this inhuman procedure was to compel the people to flee before the Americans in order that they might not learn the baseness of the constantly repeated assertion of the rebel leaders that the Americans were monsters who ate babies alive and practice other forms of hideous cruelty and unnatural vices. Upon the capture of Malolos, Aguinaldo fled to the mountain fastness of the Igorots, at Cervantes, in Northern Luzon.
Aguinaldo was captured over a year later, on March 23, 1901, by General Frederick Funston. He and the Hazzard brothers, officers in the State of Washington regiment, and two or three others, pretended to be captives, and as such they were led to Aguinaldo's retreat. As soon as the Filipino general appeared at the window of his house to look over his captives he was fired upon by the supposed prisoners, and in a few minutes the entire camp was in Gen. Funston's hands.
The Americans suffered many trying experiences, and some disasters before the revolt was stamped out. The worst was the ambush and massacre of Balangiga, Samar province, in September, 1901. Company C, Ninth Infantry, consisting of sixty-six men and three officers, was stationed there in the parish church. While the bulk of the outfit was bathing at a nearby stream one morning, with their arms stacked in the church, where the sick and wounded also were, several hundred bolomen sneaked into the church from the rear door, which some say was opened by a native priest, under pretense of coming to attend a fiesta. They murdered all those in the church and most of those who ran up when the alarm was sounded. It was a hopeless fight, as the troops were unarmed. Forty-five men and officers were killed and most of the twenty-four who escaped were wounded.
On July 1, 1901, William H. Taft, president of the second commission, was proclaimed by President McKinley civil governor of the islands. Twenty days later General MacArthur, at the request, or perhaps direction, of Governor Taft, declared twenty-four provinces to be peaceful enough to warrant the establishment of local rule there. Accordingly, civil government, after a fashion, headed for the most part by natives, was set up in most of these provinces.
Only July 4, 1902, President Roosevelt declared in a proclamation that the insurrection in the Philippines was officially at an end. Organized brigandage continued for four years thereafter.
Major General A. R. Chaffee was the last military governor of the islands. Troops were withdrawn as rapidly as possible. At the present time the American force in the islands consists of from 16,000 to 20,000 soldiers, augmented by 5,000 Philippine scouts and 4,000 Philippine constabulary. The highest number of American soldiers in the islands during the insurrection was 123,903 men and 4,135 officers. The cost of the war to the United States was $170,000,000.
Tomorrow: THE PHILIPPINES. XXXL-ARGUMENT AGAINST INDEPENDENCE.

headlines.title: The Baikans
headlines.fullText:
BY GEORGE FITCH, Auther of "At Good Old Siwash."
The Balkan states are the oldest problem in the world, next to women and politics. They are located at the jumping off end of Europe, next to Asia, and have been there on the job since the beginning of the present system of telling time. The Balkans consist principally of an extensive collection of mountains, each one of which conceals a band of patriots in the act of insurging. The Balkans have a record for battles and other bickerings which make Great Britain's early years read like the minutes of a sewing society.
The principal Balkan states are Unrest, Indignation, Insurrection, Anarchy and Chaos. Other prominent states are Roumania, Bulgaria, Servia, Montenegro and Greece. All the Balkan states together are about as big as Montana, and they have about 20,000,000 population. Crowd twenty million cowboys and western prospectors in a space the size of Montana and then give them four different kinds of religion, five languages and ten nationalities to fight about and the result would be the Balkan states in a mild form.
There are so many different nationalities in the Balkan states that a Balkan city can scarcely be told from any old New England town during a cotton mill strike. Among the most Balkan of the Balkans are the Serbo-Croats, the Bulgars and the Magyars. Generally they fight each other, and when they don't do this they unite and fight the Turks. The Balkans have been bailing on the Turk proposition since the Fifteenth century. They have licked them many times, but usually Europe steps in at the end and gives back all the marbles. The Turkish population has now been worn down to about 1,500,000, and most of these are in an unhealthy condition. The men of the Balkans wear short white skirts and long, fierce mustaches. They are not long on literature and the arts, but they are talented fighters. They have been fighting since the year 1. When there is peace in the Balkans it generally means someone has gone after a fresh supply of gunpowder.
At present the Balkan states are at their old job of licking the Turks, and they are doing it in a more workmanlike manner than ever. When they have finished it, they will probably unite and will in time take up automobiling, stock jobbing, ward politics and other evils of modern civilization, after which they will become less picturesque but considerably more prosperous.
(Copyrighted by George Mathew Adams.)

headlines.title: With the Texas Exchanges
headlines.fullText:
Volume 1, No. 1 of the Point Enterprise has been issued under the editorship of J. M. Spencer.
Fort Worth now claims to have good water. The city chemist says that by analysis it is clearly proven that filtered water from the Clear Fork of the Trinity beats their great artesian supply. This is fortunate for the city between 9:30 p. m. and 6 a. m. The Panther City always did play in luck-Dallas Times-Herald.
Who should worry?
A number of Cornell young men students have been studying domestic science and some of them may soon be accepting well paid positions as house managers. The girls want to be careful. They will steal their jobs .- San Patricio County Press.
Even a Cornell cook might do during the cotton picking season that soon will be upon us.
A refreshing shower of rain fell in our city Monday and there are good prospects for more before the week is over .- Panhandle Herald
Here's hoping Panhandle gets enough rain to give the shower all the letters coming to it.
A great scheme-especially for the autoists-is afoot. Fort Worth is to build a paved highway to the Dallas county line; Dallas to the Tarrant line. Then, with some soon roads from the government, the line will go from Fort Worth to Weatherford and on to Mineral Wells. Why not still on and on-anyway to Austin and San Antonio ?- Dallas Times-Herald.
Who ever heard of a good scheme for autoists being "afoot?"
A Fort Worth woman was arrested and fined one dollar last week for wearing abbreviated clothing. Since officers say they must follow every abbreviation per woman, they evidently hope to put a stop to the habit by seeing to it that the rule for abbreviations is observed-Crowell News.
The intention being merely to see that the abbreviation is observed, an exclamation point might do.
The city of Fort Worth, not wishing to be outdone by Dallas, or, believing that misery loves company, is going to take up the question of a new city charter .- Dallas Times-Herald.
It's safe to predict Fort Worth will not pattern after Dallas.
Simplicity is well enough, but it is one of those things of which "too much is a-plenty," and it is entirely possible to overdo it. Some day Secretary Bryan will become simple enough to offer buttermilk to the German and Russian ambassadors and then the trouble will commence-San Antonio Light.
Potlicker. Buttermilk is obsolete.
A small boy who doesn't get into a scrap once in a while has made a mistake in not being born a girl .- Cleburne Review.
Not in these days of suffragettes.
David Lamar should be admitted to the Ananias Club without delay. He is one of the contestants for the organization of its weight of gloom-Houston Post.
Lamar is refreshing. He doesn't grow to believe his own lies by repeating them.
Victoria has a few bachelors who can make more fuss over a baby in a given length of time than any bunch of widows, go 'way, child, go 'way !- Victoria Fact.
You do not mean that the bachelors go way from the widows?
The only trouble with the three-story skirt seems to be that the ground floor comes at the top .- Bonham Favorite
Showing that Bonham is ahead of Houston in fashions. Paragraphers there are still talking about the close fitting skirts.
Our legislature will be on hand in a fortnight and we feel that we shall be lucky if it doesn't prescribe the amount of salt the patriot can put on his tater .-- Houston Post.
If it will just leave him so he can buy the taters, he will be satisfied.
Is there not a British Walt Mason who can secure the laureateship through a popular vote ?- Houston Chronicle.
Imagine Kansas Walt as Royal Rhymester to King George.
If you dodge a brick and the brick breaks a plate glass window, you must pay for the window-that's if you live in New York. This is a freeman's decision. New Yorkers will have to solve the mathematical problem as to which is cheaper, a broken head or a broken window .- Austin Statesman.
It wouldn't take much figuring if everybody's head was as worthless as that of the judge who made the decision.
The new war in the Balkan peninsula is something of a puzzle to the majority of Americans, who do not understand why Bulgaria has gone to war with Servia and Greece. It is generally comprehended that they are fighting over the spoils that resulted from the successful war against Turkey, but what each nation desires is but faintly understood. San Antonio Light.
They are fighting for the drum stick.
Demand for increase of pay by Fort Worth carpenters has been acceded to by contractors, and the knight of the saw and hammer in that city now draws down $4.40 a day, and works only 44 hours a week, which gives him sixteen hours a day to his own self with a knockoff at noon on Saturdays .- Temple Telegram.
The next thing is to fix the other kind of knight of the hammer so that he won't knock more than eight hours a day.
Brides get their first intimation that "life is real" when they see the seats of the grooms' trousers-Menard Messenger.
And it is no satisfaction to them that the holes were worn out on the father's chairs while courting her.
A man in Town was soundly thrashed by his wife last week for not paying his newspaper bill after she had given him the cash to do it. May her crown be trimmed and her harp have an extra string .- Aubrey Herald.
And may her wings be feathered with ostrich plumes.
We stand ready to contribute our part toward a monument to the man who will invent a noiseless motorcycle,-Waxahachie Light.
We will not need one. He will have a halo.
The records of piscatorial prevarication in Texas reveal no lying comparable to David Lamar's. Furthermore the story of the bass that swallowed a jackrabbit near Brenham is true .- Houston Post.
Wait until the fishermen get busy on Lake Minnetonka.
David Lamar appears to take a rather humorous view of the mess he has stirred. Possibly the price of that ten thousand may have something to do with the calming impression in a short a yarn -Houston Chronicle.
A distinct impression seems to be gaining ground that his name is mis-spelled. The simplified form would make it "Liar."
The Turks must be viewing with a good deal of satisfaction the internal strife that is going on in the Balkans .- Brownwood Bulletin.
As usual the foreign brand of turkey is proving indigestible.
Paris' underground electric railroads are now carrying more than 400,000,000 passengers a year.

headlines.title: Dr. Abbott, Reactionary
headlines.fullText:
What! Is the venerable Dr. Lyman Abbott about to turn reactionary? Can he stay in the same sanctum or even in the same state with the colonel and contend that radical legislation has gone far enough?
Just listen to the following from the esteemed Outlook. The doctor may sue us under the copyright law for swiping an entire editorial, but if he does, we know the colonel will move to cut the damage down to 6 cents, in return for an extra mention of his generosity and temperate habits.
So here it is:
"America had a treaty with Russia. The two countries disagreed in their interpretation of their respective rights and duties under that treaty. America, instead of proposing to Russia to submit the interpretation of the treaty to arbitration, gave Russia notice that we annulled the treaty. It has done what it could to weaken the old ties of friendship between these two nations. America had a treaty with Japan. Japan believed that under that treaty the Japanese had a right to occupy agricultural possessions in the United States. Without waiting for conference with Japan and an endeavor to come to an agreement as to the rights of the Japanese under that treaty, California denied to Japan the rights which she supposed she possessed. America has thus done what it can to weaken the old-time friendship between Japan and America. America had a treaty with England respecting the right to use the Panama canal. America believed that under that treaty she had a right to exempt coastwise vessels from tolls. England believed that America had no such right. Instead of submitting this question of treaty interpretation to arbitration, senators now propose to refuse to continue the treaty of arbitration. If America does refuse to continue that treaty, or, continuing the treaty, does refuse to arbitrate the Panama canal question, it will do what it can to weaken the friendship between America and England. America has intimate trade relations with Germany, perhaps more intimate than with any other nation except Great Britain. It is proposing to pass a tariff measure which Germans resent because they think it denies to them privileges of trade to which they are entitled, and they propose to express that resentment by legislation aimed at American interests. Thus America is doing what it can to provoke trade hostility between America and Germany. Thus, by our careless, or our too expeditious, or our too inconsiderate action we have chilled the friendship of four great nations. We had a right to annul the treaty with Russia; we have a right to determine whom we will receive as residents within our borders; in the Outlook's opinion, we have a right to remit Panama tolls of coastwise vessels; and we do not question the right of America to pass such tariff measure as Americans think is for their interests. But no nation is so strong that it can afford to disregard the interests or feelings or even the prejudices of its neighbors, and carelessly, ignorantly and inconsiderately blunder into action which lessens their friendship if it does not arouse their hostility. It is time that the American people called a halt to their legislators, state and national."
There are thousands and thousands of informed men, patriotic, sensible and fair men, who have contended for many years that our legislators, state and national, should not be always anxious to push legislation right up to the constitutional limit line and to the utmost extent of their power. State legislatures and congress no doubt have the abstract right, even the constitutional right, to do various things that have never been done and that never should be done. Legislative bodies should learn, and if they fail to learn the people should show them, that it is not the quantity of their output that counts for the public welfare, but the quality. Legislating is a good deal like preaching. Knowing when to stop is as important as knowing how to proceed.

headlines.title: The Optimist
headlines.subtitle: BY REV. J. P. HICKS.
headlines.fullText:
CONQUERING LOVE.
"For God so loved the world that he gave his only begotten Son, that whosoever believeth in Him should not perish, but have everlasting life."-John 3:16.
Let us suppose that on a bright Sunday morning we should pick up the Bible for the first time and open it at John 3:16 and read, "For God so loved the world." And here we would stop and wonder how much God loved the world and why he loved it. And then we would read on "that he gave," and stop and consider what he gave. We would find ourselves in a state of bewilderment in our wonderings as to what God would give. It would never enter our minds that He would give anything but gold or silver or perhaps some angelic being. But in our astonishment we go forward with the reading and we fall back in amazement when we read that he gave "His Son." Here is the highest gift. Here is the unspeakable gift. In all the world there never has been a more wonderful demonstration of love than the giving of Christ to the world. This great sacrifice was made that man might be redeemed from under the curse of sin. The death sentence had been pronounced over man. Man was destined to die, and to die eternally. There was not one single ray of hope for him. The sun of hope had gone down forever. There was nothing for man to do but to grope his way in abject darkness and finally die to live no more. There was no earthly reason why the sentence should not be executed. Man had been given a fair and just trial. The verdict was death. But God, the Father of us all, looked with compassion upon man and sent His Son into the world to save from death. This is the love that passeth all understanding. This is the love that makes the angels look earthward and wonder. The beautiful thought of it all is the poor man who is wandering amid darkness and tending toward the vortex of destruction may be saved from this terrible wreck and ruin. This salvation comes as a result of looking to the Christ of Nazareth. "Look unto Me, all ye ends of the earth and be ye saved," is the gospel message to all the world.

headlines.title: ANCIENT CHURCH RESTORED.
headlines.fullText:
Of all the famous churches in London that of St. Bartholomew the Great, in Smithfield, is the most interesting and the most stimulating in the imagination. known to all students in Smithfield as "Barts." close to the hospital. Since the monastery was founded by Raherus, or Rahere in 1123, this church, which once was only the choir of the monastic church, has suffered strange vicissitudes. In the northern transept a blacksmith has his forge and a chapel a fringe factory. So well has the necessary restoration been done that to the eyes of the uninitiated it is not apparent.
The tomb of the founder is on the north of the choir. Dressed in the robes of his order, Rahere lies with folded hands and straightened limbs, in calm contentment, a monk kneeling, reading from an open Bible on each side of him, and an angel at his feet. Within the tomb his skeleton and part of a sandal were found a few years ago.
The oriel on the south side of the choir may have served as Prior Bolton's pew, or it may have been the place whence the sacristan watched the altar. In any case, it was built in the Sixteenth century.
Under the Lady chapel is a crypt, a dim and solemn place now used as a mortuary chapel .- New York Sun.
